body_txt: The Glass Cannon is a member of the Competitive Balance who is characterized by two simultaneous traits: great offense and terrible defense. They can dish it out, but they can't take it back. Their biggest strength is that they can take down their opponents easily, and their biggest weakness is that they themselves can be taken down just as easily. Occasionally referred to as a Glass Dragon, Glass Cannons are prevalent in RPGs and fighting games , as the cast needs to be big enough that "takes hits like a chump" becomes a viable character trait. This can even apply in real life. Some military weapons and vehicles can deal a lot of damage, but they're easily destroyed if they take a hit. Athletes who play contact sports can also count if they run up really good statistics but are also easily injured. Regarding Tabletop Games and Video Games , this trope is one of the most flexibles of the Competitive Balance : Make the Glass Cannon incapable of taking any punishment at all and you've got a One-Hit-Point Wonder . A matchup between two of these usually results in Rocket-Tag Gameplay . When the enemy tries to take out the Glass Cannon as fast as possible, it's Shoot the Mage First . They may have a Full Health Bonus ability as another way to encourage them to avoid getting hit. For Red Oni, Blue Oni , it can come in two flavors which can overlapse with Force and Finesse : the Red Oni promotes aggressive, reckless tactics over patience and endurance, and so can lose their cool very easily, since they're taking a lot of damage, leading to a berserker-like rage which won't last long before they're defeated or exhausted, but which can be rather dangerous for anyone in their sight who wouldn't have the time to flee during this short lapse; on the other hand, the Blue Oni is a master of the One-Hit Kill , presumably from the enemies' weak point , making all their fights Single Stroke Battles , foregoes a longer drawn-out fight in favour of a clinically practical finish , and dodges and/or parries attacks as efficiently, but if an enemy severely injures them, they will be instantly defeated physically and mentally. The exact origin of the term "glass cannon" is unclear, but it's generally agreed to have started with the early days of Dungeons & Dragons in the 1970s to describe Player Characters who couldn't take many hits, but could devastate their enemies if given the chance. It tended to refer to spellcasting classes primarily, as spellcasting classes tend to start off weak but grow exponentially in power by late-game . Opposite Trope of Stone Wall , which has very high survivability, but can't scratch any opponent. Compare and contrast Mighty Glacier , who can dish out at the expense of speed rather than toughness, and Lightning Bruiser , who can do the same without sacrificing anything (or sacrificing a "secondary" characteristic, such as range ). Not to be confused with actual cannons made of glass, which would be its Trope Namer . For those, see Glass Weapon .
